tests.prompt: My Go order service calls the Stripe SDK directly in the business logic function. Tests require a real Stripe sandbox and valid API keys. How do I restructure this so tests don't need network access?
tests.expected_behavior: Define a minimal paymentGateway interface (Charge method only) in the order package. Create external/stripe/gateway.go with StripeGateway struct wrapping the real SDK. Wire StripeGateway into order.NewService only in main.go. Write a five-line mockGateway in order_test.go. Tests pass with no network calls.
tests.expected_not: Should not suggest mocking the full Stripe SDK client or using environment variables to switch between real and fake in tests

tests.prompt: I need to add AWS S3 file uploads to my document processing service. The aws-sdk-go S3 client has 40+ methods but I only need PutObject. How should I structure the interface?
tests.expected_behavior: Define a one-method Uploader interface in the document package (consumer side), not mirroring the full S3 client. Create external/s3/gateway.go with an S3Gateway struct. Ensure the method signature uses domain/stdlib types, not *s3.PutObjectInput, to avoid leaking the SDK import into the business package.
tests.expected_not: Should not suggest defining StorageInterface in the S3 SDK wrapper package or accepting *s3.Client directly in the business function

tests.prompt: My Go training booking app is failing to compile: 'import cycle not allowed: app imports adapters imports app'. The adapters package holds the DB implementation and imports app for domain types.
tests.expected_behavior: Diagnose as a Level 1 (intra-service layer boundary) placement error. Move the repository interface into the app package as an unexported type. The direction becomes adapters → app. Show the trainingRepository interface definition in app/training_service.go with no adapters import. The concrete adapters.DB satisfies it implicitly.
tests.expected_not: Should not suggest extracting a third shared interface package or renaming packages as the fix

tests.prompt: We're adding Twilio SMS notifications to our notification service. Currently the notification logic is inside the same file as the Twilio SDK initialization. Where should the Twilio-specific code live and how do I test without sending real SMS?
tests.expected_behavior: Apply the gateway package structure: define an Emailer/SMSSender interface in the notification service package, move Twilio SDK code to external/twilio/gateway.go, write a fake in notification_test.go. Only main.go imports the Twilio SDK. Show the directory structure: notification/, external/twilio/, cmd/main.go.
tests.expected_not: Should not suggest putting the SMS interface definition in the twilio package or using build tags to swap real vs. fake

tests.prompt: Our user service has a method signature: func ProcessPayment(client *stripe.Client, amount int64). This makes the function untestable without a real Stripe client. What's the minimal change to fix this?
tests.expected_behavior: Extract the interface in the consumer package: define paymentGateway interface { Charge(amount int64, ...) } in the same package as ProcessPayment. Change the parameter to the interface type. Create a gateway file for the real Stripe implementation. Write a minimal fake for tests — the stripe.Client import disappears from the business package.
tests.expected_not: Should not suggest using reflection-based mocking or adding an optional nil-check in ProcessPayment to skip real calls in tests

tests.prompt: I'm writing a gRPC interceptor for my server and want to generate mock clients for all my service definitions using mockgen. How do I set up the generation pipeline?
tests.expected_behavior: Answer using protoc/mockgen code generation pipeline for gRPC stubs. This is about generating mocks from proto definitions, not about consumer-side interface placement.

tests.prompt: I'm the author of a Go database library and want to provide an interface that users can implement to swap out the storage backend. Should I export a StorageBackend interface from my library?
tests.expected_behavior: Note that this pattern is for application/service code; library authors are the producer, not the consumer. This skill explicitly carves out 'you are the library or SDK author' as a non-trigger. The appropriate answer involves the library exporting a struct and letting callers define their own minimal interfaces.
tests.expected_not: Should not invoke go-consumer-side-interface-gateway to recommend consumer-side placement — the asker IS the library author

tests.prompt: How do I use io.Writer as a dependency in my Go logger so I can redirect output to a file or a buffer in tests?
tests.expected_behavior: Answer by accepting io.Writer as a constructor parameter. io.Writer is a stdlib interface already minimal by design — this is a standard dependency injection pattern, not a consumer-side interface placement decision.
tests.expected_not: Should not invoke go-consumer-side-interface-gateway; io.Writer is already the minimal interface and is defined at the provider (stdlib) by design

tests.prompt: My service wraps both an internal PostgreSQL adapter (in our adapters/ package) and an external Stripe SDK. I want to apply consumer-side interface placement for both. Is the approach the same for each?
tests.expected_behavior: Explain the two structural levels: Level 1 for the internal adapter (define interface in app package, adapters/ imports app, never the reverse — import cycle prevention applies), Level 2 for Stripe (define interface in business package, real SDK lives in external/stripe/gateway.go, only main.go imports either). They use the same principle but different structural homes for the implementation.
tests.decision_axis: The Level 1 vs Level 2 conditional in I: intra-service layer boundary (adapter, same process) vs. cross-service boundary (external SDK, third-party API). The structural outcome — where the concrete implementation lives — differs.

tests.prompt: I have a minimal consumer-side interface for my Stripe gateway: type paymentGateway interface { Charge(...) }. Now I need to add Refund to the same interface. Is that a breaking change and how should I handle it?
tests.expected_behavior: Confirm this is a breaking change for all existing fakes (test doubles) that implement paymentGateway — they must add Refund. The B section of the skill identifies this as an 'interface versioning gap'. The recommended approach: define a second interface (Refunder) and pass it alongside or compose them, rather than widening the existing Charger interface. Widening defeats the minimal-interface discipline.
tests.decision_axis: The B section interface versioning gap: adding to a minimal interface breaks all fakes. Resolution: second narrow interface rather than widening the first.

tests.prompt: My business package's paymentGateway interface has this method: ProcessPayment(params *stripe.ChargeParams) (string, error). Tests still import the Stripe SDK even though I moved the interface to the consumer package. What went wrong?
tests.expected_behavior: Identify the SDK-type-leak failure mode from B: even with a consumer-side interface, if method signatures use SDK-specific types (*stripe.ChargeParams), the consumer package still imports the SDK for those types. Fix: wrap *stripe.ChargeParams in a domain type (e.g., PaymentRequest struct) at the gateway boundary. The business package interface uses only domain types; the gateway.go converts domain types to SDK types internally.
tests.decision_axis: The SDK type discipline rule in I Level 2: method signatures must use domain or stdlib types only. SDK-specific types in the signature leak the import regardless of interface placement.

tests.prompt: I'm building a microservice that has both a PostgreSQL repository (our own adapters/ package) and an external SendGrid email API. I need to decide: where do the interfaces live, where does the real SDK code live, and how do I test without hitting either?
tests.merged_advantage: The merged skill explicitly maps both dependencies to different structural levels and provides a complete recipe for each: Level 1 for PostgreSQL (interface in app/, adapters/ imports app, import cycle prevention), Level 2 for SendGrid (interface in business package, external/sendgrid/gateway.go for the SDK, main.go wires both). The named gateway package location (external/<provider>/gateway.go) is rednafi's contribution; the import-cycle enforcement argument is GWTP's contribution. Neither source alone addresses both.
tests.source1_gap: go-with-the-domain/consumer-side-interface-definition addresses intra-service adapter boundaries and import cycles but does not name the external/stripe/ gateway package structure or explain where the real external SDK code should live.
tests.source2_gap: rednafi/consumer-side-interface-segregation provides the gateway package structure and the reciprocal concrete-types rule but does not explain the import-cycle enforcement argument that makes adapter-level placement structurally necessary (not a style choice).

tests.prompt: We're replacing Stripe with Adyen in our order service. How do I make the swap affect only the wiring in main.go without touching the order package or its tests?
tests.merged_advantage: The merged skill provides the complete structural answer: because the interface is consumer-side in the order package and the implementation lives in external/stripe/gateway.go, adding external/adyen/gateway.go and updating cmd/main.go is the complete change set. This composites both the consumer-side interface placement principle (GWTP) and the named gateway-package location that makes provider swaps a one-file change (rednafi). Without naming external/<provider>/gateway.go as the canonical location, developers leave the real SDK inside the business package and the swap requires hunting through business code.
tests.source1_gap: go-with-the-domain/consumer-side-interface-definition explains why the interface belongs in the consuming package (import cycle prevention, DIP enforcement) but does not prescribe where the concrete Stripe implementation should live — 'adapters/' is named for intra-service cases but external/stripe/gateway.go is rednafi's contribution.
tests.source2_gap: rednafi/consumer-side-interface-segregation provides the gateway package structure but does not articulate the import-cycle enforcement argument that makes the consumer-side placement necessary at the intra-service layer, which is the deeper reason the order package can never import the stripe gateway package.

tests.prompt: A PR adds a StorageInterface with 12 methods to our S3 gateway package, and then imports that interface from our document service to depend on. The PR author says this is 'the interface pattern'. What's wrong with it and what should I say in the review?
tests.merged_advantage: The merged skill combines two distinct failure diagnoses: GWTP explains that defining the interface in the producer package recreates the coupling (and risks import cycles at the layer boundary); rednafi explains that a fat 12-method producer-side interface forces every consumer to implement all 12 methods in their fakes, and adding one method to the producer breaks all consumers. The review comment needs both: wrong location (should be consumer-side) AND wrong size (should be 1-2 methods from the consumer's actual call list).
tests.source1_gap: go-with-the-domain/consumer-side-interface-definition focuses on the wrong-location diagnosis and import cycle risk but does not address the fat-interface problem — 12 methods is not called out as a distinct failure mode.
tests.source2_gap: rednafi/consumer-side-interface-segregation explicitly names fat producer-side interfaces as failure mode (ce13) and the 12-method coupling problem, but does not articulate the import-cycle structural argument that applies when the interface lives in adapters/ and both the consumer and producer import it.
